question.text: Meera's portfolio has 60% in Fund A (return 14%, standard deviation 20%) and 40% in Fund B (return 8%, standard deviation 10%). The correlation between A and B is 0.50. What is the portfolio's standard deviation (approximately)?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Portfolio variance = w₁²σ₁² + w₂²σ₂² + 2w₁w₂ρσ₁σ₂ = (0.36 × 400) + (0.16 × 100) + (2 × 0.6 × 0.4 × 0.50 × 20 × 10) = 144 + 16 + 48 = 208. Standard deviation = √208 = 14.42%. **The Trap:** 16.00% is the weighted-average standard deviation (0.6 × 20 + 0.4 × 10 = 16%), which ignores the diversification benefit — this would only be correct if correlation were +1.0. 12.65% assumes zero correlation (√(144 + 16) = √160 = 12.65%). 15.60% uses an incorrect correlation of 0.80. **Cross-Exam Context:** Two-asset portfolio variance is a core concept tested in NISM Series V-A and X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The weighted-average std dev is the MAXIMUM possible portfolio risk (when ρ = +1). Any ρ < 1 gives diversification benefit, reducing actual risk below that ceiling.
